items.q: Does the Trinity mean Christians worship three gods?
items.correct: No - Christianity is monotheist: the Trinity is one God in three persons, and belief in three separate gods (tritheism) is rejected as heresy.
items.misconception: The Trinity means Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to the three wise men at Jesus's birth.
items.why: 'Three persons' sounds like three gods to students; the one-substance claim is exactly what the doctrine exists to protect.

items.q: Do Christians believe Jesus rose only as a ghost or spirit?
items.correct: No - mainstream teaching holds Jesus rose bodily: Luke 24 stresses that he ate food and could be touched, though his risen body was transformed and glorified.
items.misconception: The resurrection means Jesus's ghost appeared to the disciples.
items.random_wrong: Christians believe Jesus rose as an angel with wings.
items.why: Bodily resurrection distinguishes the Christian claim from mere survival stories, and exam answers often blur it into a vague 'spirit'.

items.q: Do Christians teach that being nice automatically earns heaven?
items.correct: No - salvation is taught as God's gift of grace received through faith, with good works flowing from it; denominations differ on details, but 'earning heaven by niceness' misstates the doctrine.
items.misconception: Christianity teaches that anyone who is kind enough earns a place in heaven.
items.random_wrong: Christians believe heaven is only for clergy.
items.why: Grace versus works is a core GCSE distinction; the 'good people go to heaven' shortcut hides what the tradition actually claims.

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: One God in three persons: Father, Son and Holy Spirit
items.random_wrong: Three names God uses at different times in history
items.why: Correct answer: One God in three persons: Father, Son and Holy Spirit. “Three separate gods working together” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.random_wrong: The ascension of Jesus into heaven
items.why: It's God became human in Jesus. “Jesus's baptism in the Jordan” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: According to the creeds, on which day did Jesus rise from the dead?
items.why: Correct answer: The third day. “The same day he died” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The reconciliation of God and humanity through Jesus's death
items.random_wrong: The building of the first churches
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The reconciliation of God and humanity through Jesus's death. “The forty days of Lent” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
